text: Official livestock-health services are the right escalation path for severe, spreading, or uncertain animal-health problems. PashuPulse should act as first-line support, not as a replacement for trained animal-health services.
FMD- or brucellosis-like concerns should be treated as herd or public livestock-health issues and routed to official animal-health support. Do not diagnose these diseases from text alone.
Farmer-level first aid should focus on preventing worsening, keeping the animal calm, observing signs, and seeking trained help when needed. It should remain supportive, not invasive.

Moldy, damp, clumped, or bad-smelling feed should be treated as suspicious and should not be casually fed to dairy animals. Feed contamination concerns should focus on dry storage and removing suspicious feed.
Left-sided tight belly, restlessness, fast breathing, or severe abdominal distension after feed can be urgent in cattle or buffalo and should trigger trained-help escalation.
For a healthy calf slow to start dry feed, first check feed freshness, water access, comfort, and whether the calf remains alert and active.
Escalate calf feeding issues when the calf becomes weak, stops drinking, has ongoing diarrhea, or cannot stand normally. Do not turn this into medicine or ORS dosing advice.
Clean milk production depends on clean animals, clean hands, clean utensils, and hygienic milking practices.
Abnormal milk such as visible clots, blood-like appearance, or bad smell should be kept separate while trained guidance is sought if red flags appear. Do not guarantee milk safety.
Dairy animals need reliable access to clean drinking water. Water-intake changes should be interpreted together with feed, heat, behavior, dung, and other health signs.
Do not diagnose disease from drinking more or less water alone. Ask one key check and escalate only if red flags such as severe weakness, high fever, breathing trouble, or severe diarrhea appear.
Routine cattle care includes observing appetite, water, dung, standing or walking, cud chewing, milk change, and behavior.
Calving or calf-care contexts should stay clean, calm, and non-invasive unless trained help is present. Do not teach repositioning or hard pulling.
